coredata.dc:title: Origin of the lithium metal anode instability in solid-state batteries during discharge
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Dheeraj Kumar
Fuchs, Till
Krempaszky, Christian
Schweitzer, Pascal
Lerch, Christian
Richter, Felix H.
Janek, Jürgen
coredata.dc:description: Enabling the lithium metal anode (LMA) in solid-state batteries (SSBs) would increase energy density and specific energy compared with lithium-ion batteries. However, pore formation in LMAs with irregular morphology, even at low current density, during discharge results in an unstable, high-impedance interface. Understanding and addressing this inherent anode instability is essential for increasing the power densities in SSBs. Herein, we suggest that the morphology of the stripped electrode is related to dislocations in the LMA. To investigate the influence of dislocations, symmetric cells, LiǀLi6.25Al0.25La3Zr2O12(LLZO)ǀX-Li, are studied, where X-Li represents the microstructurally controlled LMA obtained via suitable thermomechanical processing. Operando impedance measurements are corroborated with SEM, confocal microscopy, and AFM data. Based on the experimental observations, a mechanism for pore formation is proposed. We show that the stack pressure required to maintain a stable interface is governed by the lithium microstructure and its thermomechanical processing history.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of pore morphology on impedance
Influence of pore morphology on stress required to re-attain interfacial contact
Microstructural evolution of fine-/coarse-grained Li during fabrication
Charge-transfer kinetics at unstrained vs. strained lattices
Mass-transport in unstrained vs. strained lattice regions
Relative strengths (σ y) of unstrained vs. strained lattice regions
Correlating pore morphology with dislocation topology
Microstructural changes on annealing at T H ≈ 0.99
Softening mechanism: Recrystallization and grain growth
Charge extraction and time to reach the cutoff potential

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrating the fabrication and the electrochemical measurement protocol for the symmetric Li cell having microstructurally controlled LMA WEs (topology, density, and distribution)

(A–F) The lithium symmetric cells were fabricated using fine- and coarse-grained lithium at an isostatic stress of 362 MPa. A plastic strain of ∼16% is introduced involving significant increase in dislocation density, i.e., strain hardening resulting in LiidǀLLZOǀSG/LG-LiWE. SG-/LG-Li refers to strained state of fine-/coarse-grained lithium. (B) Thus, simultaneous control over (C) dislocation length, being proportional to grain size (indicated by curved red lines for mixed character type single dislocation between the grain surface to GB and GB to GB), and (D) dislocation density with uniform distribution is achieved. Thermal treatment of (B) leads to (E) LiidǀLLZOǀh-SG/h-LG-LiWE, having (F) lower dislocation density along with non-uniform distribution and larger grain size. Dislocation pinning during annealing is indicated by the red dotted circle in (F).
SEM images

(A and B) SEM images of (A) fine-grained polycrystalline lithium (fine-Li), and (B) coarse-grained polycrystalline lithium (coarse-Li) foils. It is to be noted that SG-/LG-Li refers to the strained state of fine-/coarse-grained lithium (Figure 1).
Electrochemical data

(A) Selected Nyquist plots at different times of continuous galvanostatic electrochemical impedance spectra (GEIS) of LiidǀLLZOǀSG/LG-Li and LiidǀLLZOǀh-SG/h-LG-Li symmetric cells at 0.1 mA cm−2. The equivalent circuit used for fitting is also indicated (R, resistor; Q, constant phase element).
(B and C) Temporal evolution of (B) potential and (C) interfacial resistance (RInt) during GEIS measurements. Note: the initial geometrical electrode area was used for current normalization.
SEM images of the different thermomechanically processed, unidirectionally stripped LMAs
(A–D) (A) LG-Li, (B) h-LG-Li, (C) SG-Li, and (D) h-SG-Li. The images are shown with increasing magnification from left to right.
High- and low-magnification images indicating surface topographical features of stripped LMAs
(A–D) AFM images of stripped (A) LG-Li, (B) h-LG-Li, (C) SG-Li, and (D) h-SG-Li LMAs.
(E–H) Confocal images of stripped (E) LG-Li, (F) h-LG-Li, (G) SG-Li, and (H) h-SG-Li LMAs at 20× magnification.
Evolution of Nyquist plots with increasing stress

(A and B) Nyquist plots for the stripped (A) SG-Li and (B) LG-Li LMAs as the applied stress, σ applied, is increased from 0.2 to 3.0 MPa (indicated by the arrows). A decrease in constriction impedance, R Int, is observed showing contact re-formation.
(C) The fitted data for R Int vs. σ applied.
Frequency dependence of the potential lines and the effect of pore morphology on time constants
(A and B) (A) Relatively homogeneous and (B) inhomogeneous stripping occurs in LG-Li and h-LG-Li, respectively.
(A) At high frequency, shallow pores conduct dielectrically, whereas at low frequency they act as high-impedance elements (as f → 0 then ZC → ∞) resulting in constriction at the depleted contacts.

(B) The red dotted line indicates the height threshold above which the pores cease to conduct dielectrically at low frequencies resulting in effective reduction of h-LG-Li electrode area. Equipotential lines are depicted in dashed orange, whereas current pathways are depicted in arrowed black lines.

(C and D) Initial Nyquist spectra for LG-Li and h-LG-Li (light blue and orange) superimposed with that at 0.7 V (dark blue and orange). The dotted boxed high-frequency region in (C) is expanded in (D).
Proposed mechanism for pore formation in LMA
(A) An isostatic stress of 362 MPa during fabrication leads to ∼16% plastic strain accompanying increase in microstructural energy of LMA.

(B) Dislocation density (shown by nodal network) is expected to increase during this step, whereas the increased vacancy concentration quickly reaches thermal equilibrium (indicated by dotted circles). The strained lattice surrounding the dislocation is indicated in orange and the unstrained lattice is shown in gray. These regions will differ locally in their yield strengths (σ y), as shown in (C).

(C) Smaller R CT leads to faster electrodissolution of the strained lattice. Both adhesion and creep maintain interfacial contact as indicated by the magnified view of an interface region. The pore surface enables surface diffusion, as indicated by filled gray circles.

(D) Annihilation of LEDS (formed during continuous recovery/recrystallization) at the interface leads to pore surface roughening; indicated by the magnified view of a pore|lithium interface region (bold orange circle). Also, surface atom rearrangement to form low energy facets (e.g., (100) for Li) can also lead to roughening; indicated by the transport of surface atoms to the vacancy (red dotted circle) in (C). In addition, growing pore depth leads to progressively decreasing mass transport per unit length and time as indicated by the differences in arrow lengths in (C) and (D). Stress amplification at the depleted contacts with growing pore depth results in either power-law breakdown or slip-mediated plastic yielding.
SEM images, confocal images, and microstructural evolution of LMAs on annealing
(A) Low-magnification SEM image of stripped h-LG-Li LMA.
(B) High-magnification image of a representative pit indicated by the dotted area in (A).
(C and D) (C) Confocal image of a random pit in h-LG-Li. The corresponding depth profile along the blue plane in (C) is shown in (D).
(E–H) Corresponding data for h-SG-Li.

(I–L) Scheme of the processes occurring during microstructural evolution of LMAs on annealing. (I) Uniform distribution of dislocations in SG-/LG-Li. (J) Recovery leading to low-energy dislocation structure (LEDS) formation during polygonization and subsequent recrystallization (K). The lighter areas indicated by a black dotted circle in (K) represent the strain-free matrix. Recrystallization followed by grain growth (L) leading to the microstructure of h-SG-Li/h-LG-Li. The gray and the black lines are the GBs of pre- and post-annealed samples. Also shown is the sessile forest dislocation.
Correlation of extracted charge with dislocation length and thus grain size

The left vertical axis shows the extracted charge as a function of microstructurally controlled LMAs. The right vertical axis shows the theoretically calculated extracted lithium thickness assuming layer-by-layer removal. The total electrode thickness is assumed to be 100 μm with a capacity of 5.816 mAh. Higher charge is extracted from LMAs with larger grain size, which is associated with relatively non-uniform strip ping, and vice versa.

head.ce:title: Origin of the lithium metal anode instability in solid-state batteries during discharge

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enabling the lithium metal anode (LMA) in solid-state batteries (SSBs) would increase energy density and specific energy compared with lithium-ion batteries. However, pore formation in LMAs with irregular morphology, even at low current density, during discharge results in an unstable, high-impedance interface. Understanding and addressing this inherent anode instability is essential for increasing the power densities in SSBs. Herein, we suggest that the morphology of the stripped electrode is related to dislocations in the LMA. To investigate the influence of dislocations, symmetric cells, LiǀLi6.25Al0.25La3Zr2O12(LLZO)ǀX-Li, are studied, where X-Li represents the microstructurally controlled LMA obtained via suitable thermomechanical processing. Operando impedance measurements are corroborated with SEM, confocal microscopy, and AFM data. Based on the experimental observations, a mechanism for pore formation is proposed. We show that the stack pressure required to maintain a stable interface is governed by the lithium microstructure and its thermomechanical processing history.

Maintaining a stable Liǀsolid electrolyte (SE) interface is critical to achieving fast-(dis)charging solid-state batteries (SSBs). However, pore formation in the lithium metal anode (LMA) results in unstable, high-impedance interface even at low current density during discharge. It also increases the required pressure thresholds in SSBs. Therefore, understanding and addressing anode instability is key to achieving the fast-charging electric vehicle (EV) goal. Herein, we show that the thermomechanical processing history that led to the microstructural evolution of non-equilibrium defects (dislocations) in LMA plays a critical role in controlling the pore morphology in SSBs. Furthermore, we propose that pore formation is a complex process involving multiple phenomena.

Pores with irregular morphology inevitably form in lithium metal anode (LMA), even at low current density during discharge, resulting in a high-impedance solidǀsolid interface. It is proposed herein that the pore formation is a multi-factorial effect and depends on the thermomechanical processing history of LMA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data generated during the study are available from the lead contact upon reasonable request. This study did not generate a code.

ce:section.ce:para:
The increasing trend toward renewable energy sources has accelerated research in secondary energy storage systems with high energy density, particularly in solid-state batteries (SSBs). 1
,
2
,
3 Lithium metal anodes (LMAs) combined with inorganic solid electrolytes are expected to increase both the energy density (3,860 mAh g−1 and −3.04 V versus standard hydrogen electrode) and the power density of SSBs compared with conventional Li-ion batteries. 2
,
4 In addition, thermal runaway issues associated with flammable organic liquid electrolytes may be overcome, promising greater safety and longer life. 5
,
6
,
7
,
8

However, the rate capability of SSBs is generally still lower compared with cells with liquid electrolytes. 3
,
9 One of the contributing factors is the instability of Liǀsolid electrolyte (SE) interfaces, particularly the microstructure of the solid electrolyte (SE) and its influence on lithium electrodeposition (plating). 10
,
11
,
12
,
13
,
14
,
15 Microstructural features of the Li|SE interface determine the maximum current density or the critical current density (CCD) that a system can withstand before a short circuit. 16 Another critical factor is the rapid pore formation in the LMA, causing reduced electrode-electrolyte contact area during anodic stripping. 17
,
18
,
19 This leads to rapid overvoltage increase due to the buildup of local constriction resistances. 20
,
21
,
22 In fact, the CCD for stripping has been shown to be lower than the CCD for plating. 23

Inhomogeneous electrodissolution or stripping leads to a porous interface in LMAs. 24 As a result, the electrode surface gets rough. This undesirable factor increases the stack pressure thresholds in SSBs due to roughness-induced interfacial stress amplification and strain hardening. 25
,
26
,
27 In addition, different strain rates will occur at the depleted contacts depending on the area of each contact. This can result in a rapid and inhomogeneous ductile-to-brittle transition of the contacted lithium metal at the interface, as body-centered cubic (bcc) structures are sensitive to strain rates. 28 In the long term, this can manifest itself in an increase in pressure thresholds and thus contribute to technical challenges in SSBs.

Therefore, it is important to understand the basic mechanism of pore formation. The low self-diffusivity (D Li ∼ 10−11 cm2 s−1) of bulk lithium at room temperature, which cannot keep up with the imposed current (at or above a certain critical current threshold), is attributed to pore formation. 17 This is confirmed by the more stable performance of alloy-based anodes, which increase the (effective) diffusivity of lithium by a factor of three for the case of Li0.9Mg0.1 compared to pure lithium. 29 However, the observed electrode morphology cannot be solely attributed to the low self-diffusivity of lithium alone. A spatially uniform Li diffusivity would lead to uniform stripping, i.e., layer-by-layer lithium removal at the interface. This suggests a complex mass transport (diffusion) coupled charge-transfer process in the LMA and at the LiǀSE interface, respectively.

Polycrystalline microstructures contain several structural defects such as grain boundaries (2D), dislocations (1D), and point defects (0D). 30 The structural defects are associated with individual (enhanced) charge-transfer and mass transport kinetics compared with the ideal or defect-free lattice, in particular in the case of 1D and 2D defects. 31
,
32
,
33
,
34
,
35
,
36 It is well known that the self-diffusivities are different along each of these defects, typically with D S ≥ D GB ≥ D D > D V (S, grain surface; GB, grain boundary; D, dislocations; V, bulk). 28
,
37 Thus, highly inhomogeneous kinetics and resulting local fluxes are expected across the interface in response to the imposed current on account of the different local microstructures. Consideration of these defect types is even more indispensable in SSBs where thin Li electrodes (<30 μm) 1 will be employed. The lithium electrode undergoes significant microstructural changes (i.e., an increase in dislocation density) during mechanical or thermomechanical processing. 38 In effect, lithium anodes will have significantly different mechanical and chemical properties compared with bulk lithium depending on their treatment and their cycling history. The role of dislocations in pore formation at parent metal|SE anodes has already been demonstrated for single crystalline Ag anodes, where the pore density after stripping correlates well with the dislocation density and where complex oscillatory behavior results from the electrochemo-mechanical coupling. 39
,
40
,
41

Herein, we show that the dislocation density, topology (network structure and dislocation loops), 28 and distribution dictate the texture of the stripped LMA. Via appropriate mechanical and thermomechanical processing, we achieved control over the pristine lithium microstructure. The electrochemical performance of anodes with different microstructures was then evaluated in Li symmetric cells with Li6.25Al0.25La3Zr2O12 (LLZO) as SE using operando impedance measurements under unidirectional current load of 0.1 mA cm−2.

The extracted capacities and the temporal impedance evolution are then corroborated with the SEM, confocal microscopy, and AFM measurements. We then propose a mechanism based on the effect of enhanced kinetics at dislocations, dislocation topology and distribution, surface diffusion, adhesion, microstructural recovery/recrystallization, plastic anisotropy, and creep to account for the complex pore morphology observed during LMA stripping.

To evaluate the influence of lithium microstructure, i.e., grain size, dislocation topology (i.e., either mixed character or Frank-Read type [concentric dislocation loops], dislocation density, and distribution), symmetric Li cells, i.e., LiidǀLLZOǀSG/LG-LiWE, were fabricated at an isostatic stress of 362 MPa, as shown in Figure 1 (see supplemental experimental procedures for details). Liid represents the reference/counter electrode, free of constriction and charge-transfer resistances, 17 and SG/LG-LiWE are the microstructurally controlled working electrodes (WEs). All the lithium samples underwent similar cleaning/processing procedures to obtain respective foils as detailed in supplemental experimental procedures. LLZO was chosen as SE as it is kinetically stable and has negligible interfacial charge-transfer resistance (R CT) against lithium. 42 LLZO was synthesized according to a previous report from our group. 43 Thermal annealing of LiidǀLLZOǀSG/LG-LiWE resulted in the LiidǀLLZOǀh-SG/h-LG-LiWE cell (where SG stands for “fine/small grained," LG stands for “coarse/large grained,” and the prefix “h” refers to heated or the annealed state), having altered WE microstructure (Figure 1).

Figure 2 shows scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of fine- and coarse-grained lithium foils (see the supplemental experimental procedures for details on sample preparation). The mean linear intercept method gave mean in-plane 44 grain sizes of 20 and 160 μm for fine- and coarse-grained foils, respectively.

Subsequently, these electrodes were investigated in a symmetric cell setup, i.e., LiidǀLLZOǀX-Li (X refers to SG-Li, LG-Li, h-SG-Li, and h-LG-Li WEs). Herein, Liid acts as a reference electrode/counter electrode with negligible interfacial resistance. 42 During stripping of the WEs, Liid is also morphologically stable, free of constriction effects, 20
,
21
,
22
,
45
,
46 and does not contribute to the observed cell impedance. Therefore, any observed impedance in the low-frequency range (∼1 kHz) is solely attributed to the X-Li WEs.

Lithium stripping experiments were performed at 0.1 mA cm−2 via continuous galvanostatic electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements with an amplitude of 0.01 mA cm−2 and a cutoff potential of 1.0 V (Figure 3 A) and negligible stack pressure. To evaluate the changes during stripping, the impedance evolution at selected times is shown in the Nyquist representation in Figure 3A. All four cell types show two semicircles in the initial impedance spectra and one progressively emerging semicircle during stripping. Using their respective apex frequencies, these are related to bulk transport (4 MHz), grain boundary transport (50 kHz), and interfacial transport (∼1 kHz), which agrees well with the literature. 17 For the fit, a series connection of three parallel R-Q elements was used, where R and Q are a resistor and a constant phase element, respectively. This equivalent circuit is shown schematically in the upper part of Figure 3A.

In all the Nyquist plots shown, the contributions of the bulk and the grain boundaries are largely constant. However, the interfacial contribution increases slowly at the beginning of the stripping, followed by a very rapid increase >2 kΩ cm2, which mainly contributes to the overvoltage visible in Figure 3B. The more gradual increase in overvoltage for h-LG-Li can also be seen in the Nyquist plots, as these show a more gradual occurrence of the interfacial contribution than, for example, LG-Li.

The voltage profiles during stripping of the electrodes (X-Li) are shown in Figure 3B. The general stripping behavior appears to be similar for all four types of electrodes, with a stable voltage plateau followed by a steep voltage rise, indicating severe voiding and contact loss. However, the stripping capacity varies with each type of anode, increasing for SG-Li < h-SG-Li < h-LG-Li < LG-Li. Interestingly, the shape of the voltage profile also changes for h-LG-Li, showing a less steep and more gradual increase in overvoltage. It is interesting to compare the present result with our previous work wherein we investigated two different grain sizes of lithium, i.e., 20 and 295 μm at higher stack pressures of 0.2 and 2.0 MPa under similar electrochemical conditions (0.1 mA cm−2 with a cutoff 1.0 V). 43 As expected, the time to reach cutoff voltage is increased (at least 36 h) for all the samples except for the SG-Li (denoted as c-Li in the previous work). It took ∼10.0 h to reach a cutoff voltage of 1.0 V at 0.2 MPa in the previous study compared with ∼13 h observed herein. Since the same sample was used, the observed discrepancy can be attributed to the microstructural relaxation of the stored lithium, which is discussed in more depth in the following sections, “mechanism of pore formation,” “pore formation: a multi-factorial effect,” and “correlating pore morphology with dislocation topology.”

Since LLZO is kinetically stable against lithium, 42 the low-frequency contribution is attributed to contact loss at the interface leading to constriction resistance. 21
,
22
,
45
,
46
,
47
,
48 Therefore, to evaluate the morphology of the LMA interfaces during stripping, we performed SEM analyses, and the results were compared with the observed impedance changes and the extracted capacities. Interestingly, the electrodes were easily detachable from the LLZO after severe voiding had taken place until an overvoltage of 1.0 V vs. the Liid electrode.

Figures 4A–4D show the SEM images of stripped LMAs at different magnifications. The textural features of each LMA are different and distinct from each other. After stripping, LG-Li shows a homogeneous and rather sharp distribution of pores of about 10–15 μm. Both SG-Li and h-SG-Li exhibit relatively smoother surface features compared with LG-Li/h-LG-Li.

Therefore, to quantify the textural parameters, we performed atomic force microscopy (AFM) and confocal microscopy (Figure 5 ). Figures 5A–5D show the AFM surface topographies of stripped X-Li electrodes. The high-magnification AFM data show that the textures of each sample are distinct and different, which is consistent with the SEM images (Figure 4). Interestingly, the root-mean-square roughness (S q) of the stripped lithium anodes (S q (LG-Li) = 0.917
±
0.084 μm, S q (SG-Li) = 0.768
±
0.041 μm, S q (h-LG-Li) = 0.833
±
0.174 μm, S q (h-SG-Li) = 0.822
±
0.075 μm), were found to be quite close. We also evaluated the maximum height S z as roughness descriptor and the values were also found to be similar. Clearly, a single descriptor is insufficient to describe the textural features completely, particularly at high magnification. 49 As roughness is a function of magnification, we recorded low-magnification (20×) confocal microscopy images to get further insight into the textural characteristics. 25

Figures 5E–5H show the confocal images of the Li-X electrodes at 20× magnification. Figures 5E and 5H reveal the presence of random pits in annealed electrodes, i.e., h-LG/h-SG-Li that are absent in LG/SG-Li. S q of the stripped electrodes were determined to be: S q (LG-Li) = 1.80
±
0.15 μm, S q (SG-Li) = 1.00
±
0.19 μm, S q (h-LG-Li) = 5.7
±
0.70 μm S q (h-SG-Li) = 5.31
±
0.25 μm, indicating higher roughness for the annealed electrodes.

The effect of pore morphology on the stress required to re-form contact was evaluated and is shown in Figures 6A and 6B. Figures 6A and 6B show the decrease in constriction impedance for SG-/LG-Li (stripped until 1.0 V) as the vertically applied stress (σ applied) is increased from 0.2 to 3.0 MPa (indicated by the arrow). The fitted data for R Int vs. σ applied are shown in Figure 6C. The measurements were performed immediately after the load was increased, which was then held constant during the data acquisition. Clearly, there is a decrease in R Int for both the stripped LMAs, although different contact areas were achieved for the same applied stress (ΔR Int for a given σ applied), but the R Int/σ applied trend is similar. If we ignore the first data point for LG-Li then it can be roughly divided into three regions depending on its slope (Figure 6C), which is discussed later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pore morphology on impedance
ce:section.ce:para:
The different pore morphology also manifests itself in the measured impedance of the respective cells. To explain this, the origin of the interfacial resistance must be clarified first. It has been shown that neither charge transfer nor a resistive SEI is responsible for the interfacial impedance observed at the (Li2CO3 free) LiǀLLZO interface. 17 For this interface, R Int is a purely geometric phenomenon, i.e., the constriction effect and the resulting constriction impedance due to current focusing at depleted or point contacts (R constriction) occurs at low frequencies. 20
,
21
,
22
,
45 If σ ion is the ionic conductivity of the SE and d contact is the diameter of each of the N non-interfering contacts, obtained upon progressive stripping, then impedance of such a geometric ensemble at low frequency is given by 46
(Equation 1)
R
c o n s t r i c t i o n
=
R Int
≈
1 /
(
2 N
d
c o n t a c t
σ
i o n
)
where both N and d contact are decreasing functions of stripping time. Therefore, the distribution and the contact geometry, which is determined by the pore morphology (size and depth), affects the interfacial resistance even if the total contact area is the same in different cases. 21 For example, for any given R Int or potential the product N‧d contact is constant, but different combinations of N and d contact can lead to the same R Int. Therefore, in Figure 6 we compare the effects of pore morphology on the impedance behavior of LG-Li, a case of homogeneous stripping, with h-LG-Li (inhomogeneous stripping, Figures 4A, 4B, 5E, and 5F).

As the current constriction is a frequency-dependent phenomenon, two cases each (low and high frequency) must be considered when investigating the impedance behavior of electrodes with homogeneous and inhomogeneous voiding. These cases are shown in Figures 7A and 7B, where the dashed orange lines represent equipotential lines, while the current distribution is represented by the black lines. The initial impedance behavior (at t ≈ 0 s, no-voiding) is compared with that at 0.7 V (t >> 0 s, significant voiding) for LG-Li and h-LG-Li (Figures 7C and 7D). At the same overpotential (0.7 V), a larger amount of charge has been extracted from LG-Li compared with h-LG-Li (Figure 3B).

At high frequencies, homogeneously distributed and shallow pores (Figure 5E) conduct dielectrically, indicating that the impedance contributions in the high-frequency range do not depend on the state of pore formation (Figure 7A). 21 This is the case for bulk and grain boundary impedance for LG-Li, as shown in light and dark blue in Figures 7A, 7C, and 7D, which is almost constant regardless of pore formation. On the other hand, very wide and deep pores, as visible for h-LG-Li (Figure 5F, also discussed in detail later), lead to an effective reduction of the electrode area, which slightly increases the contribution of bulk and grain boundary (Figures 7B–7D). This is indicated by the red dotted line in Figure 7B, the height threshold above which the pores cease to conduct dielectrically at high frequencies, leading to an effective reduction in electrode area. This explains the increase in impedance in the high-frequency regions of the Nyquist plot visible in light and dark orange.

At low frequencies, pores always lead to current focusing at the interface and thus to an additional impedance contribution due to current constriction. The relaxation time of the process depends strongly on the geometry and the size of the constriction zone. Since the constriction effect is more homogeneous when the pores are distributed homogeneously, the corresponding semicircle in the Nyquist plot for LG-Li has a more defined shape. However, when the pores are inhomogeneous, the relaxation time distribution of the constriction phenomenon will be broader, resulting in a wider and drawn-out contribution in the Nyquist plot for h-LG-Li (Figures 7C and 7D). This broadening is observed for h-LG-Li in dark orange (at 0.7 V), which shows a very depressed semicircle compared with LG-Li. This also explains that impedance fitting with only one R-Q element representing the constriction may become erroneous when highly inhomogeneous voiding occurs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pore morphology on stress required to re-attain interfacial contact
ce:section.ce:para:
Figure 6C shows that even at σ applied = 0.2 MPa (lower than σ y = 0.8 MPa for bulk lithium, and ∼16 MPa for strain hardened Li), R Int decreases in both cases. This is due to contact stresses exceeding σ y due to stress amplification at the depleted contacts (Figure 4). 50
,
51 Thus, it is primarily the local deformation of pillared Li at the interface. Since roughness is a multiple length scale phenomenon, this can be taken to be a higher wavelength contact over the LLZO surface. 25 Interestingly, R Int for the stripped LG-Li decreases much faster when the applied stress is increased from 0.2 to 0.3 MPa, as indicated by the dotted region in Figure 6C.

The deeper pores with much higher contact stresses result in higher strain rates (
ε ˙
) at the contacted Li pillars in the stripped LG-Li, resulting in faster and more deformation. The effect of this differential
ε ˙
can be seen in comparison between constriction components with increasing stress (Figures 6A and 6B). The constriction semicircle is slightly more depressed for stripped LG-Li compared with stripped SG-Li, indicating that the complex pore geometry 52 leads to a complex contact geometry with increasing stress (Figures 4A and 4C). This is also confirmed by the low-frequency data of the constriction signal, which show a continuous decrease that can be attributed to continued plastic flow (dashed circle in Figure 6B).

This increase in contact stresses likely results in a constant slope (R Int/σ applied) up to 1.0 MPa (region 1, Figure 6C). Thereafter, the slope decreases significantly up to 2.0 MPa for both stripped LMAs, indicating an increasing difficulty in plastic flow (region 2). This can be due to the continuous increase in dislocation density up to 1.0 MPa, leading to strain hardening thereafter. 28
,
53 Also, unlike region 1, both bulk and interfacial deformation of Li are expected.

Between 2.5 and 3.0 MPa (region 3), the slope is smaller compared with the other two regions, with only a slight decrease in R Int. This indicates that plastic flow is further impeded due to the previous strain hardening. Moreover, we believe that both dynamic recovery and micromechanical strengthening of Li 54 (due to LLZO surface roughness) is operational here, which explains the nearly constant R Int values. It is argued that the intimate contacts with roughness at shorter wavelength becomes progressively more difficult and higher stress is required to effect further contact. 25
,
53
,
55
,
56 The length-scale-dependent nature of the interfacial contact decisively influences the evolution of the local microstructure and sub-surface plastic deformation due to the complex local stress state in Li. Generally, it can be assumed that additional geometrically necessary dislocations are formed in lithium sub-surface regions. Due to roughness on small scales, the size-effect of plasticity plays a key role and has to be taken into account. 53 In addition, both self-fixture or mechanical anchoring at higher wavelength and increasing friction with increasing contact area is further expected to increase flow stress. 57
,
58 This explains why increasing stress has a diminishing effect on R Int. Also, adhesion is likely to play a key role when the contact is established even at shorter wavelength roughness. However, the detailed interfacial contact analysis incorporating the effect of length-scale-dependent plasticity, friction, and adhesion is a challenging task and beyond the scope of this study. 53
,
58
,
59
,
60
,
61 Finally, Figure 6C indicates that R Int for a given σ applied is always higher for stripped SG-Li compared with stripped LG-Li. This is due to higher strength of SG-Li compared with LG-Li on account of the combined effect of grain boundary strengthening and strain hardening and is discussed in “microstructural evolution of fine-/coarse-grained Li during fabrication.”

If the removal of a layer of lithium atoms with the thickness of a lattice unit (q ≈ 72.24 ‧ 10−6 mAh cm−2) would lead to the complete loss of the interfacial contact, it can be shown that at 0.1 mA cm−2 it would take ∼2.6 s to reach this state with zero applied load, i.e., σ applied = 0. However, the capacity of at least 0.36 mAh (for SG-Li) is extracted via the slowly receding interfacial contact (>10 h). This implies that the contact is maintained even at longer times (>10 h, Figure 3B) by complex dynamic interfacial processes. This is due to the correlative effects of interfacial adhesion, creep, relative anode strengths (microstructural changes during thermo-mechanical processing), and stress amplification at the progressively depleting contacts, i.e., plastic yielding. This is discussed in detail below and thereafter correlated with a proposed mechanism for pore formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructural evolution of fine-/coarse-grained Li during fabrication
ce:section.ce:para:
During isostatic compression (362 MPa), ∼16% plastic strain is introduced into the working electrodes, indicating significant increase in dislocation density (strain hardening). As a result, both the microstructural energy and the yield strength (σ y) of the final electrode, i.e., SG-Li and LG-Li, is expected to increase. Including the effect of the grain size, i.e., grain boundary or Hall-Petch strengthening, the overall strengthening can then be described by: 28
(Equation 2)
σ y
=
σ i
+
k y
d
− 1 ∕ 2
+ α G b
ρ
where
σ i
is the inherent lattice friction or Peierls-Nabarro stress, 62
k y
is the strengthening coefficient,
d
is the mean grain size,
α
is a material constant and depends on the crystal structure and dislocation substructure, G is the shear modulus,
b
is the magnitude of the Burgers vector, and
ρ
is the dislocation density. The first two terms on the right-hand side of Equation 2 represent grain boundary strengthening, 63
,
64 whereas the last term indicates strain hardening. 28 Based on Equation 2, SG-Li is expected to have higher strength compared with LG-Li, i.e.,
σ y
(SG-Li) >
σ y
(LG-Li) as
ρ
∝
1/d. 65
,
66

The grains in the strained electrode, i.e., SG-/LG-Li, will experience non-uniform plastic deformation due to crystallographic anisotropy. 67 To provide displacement compatibility between differently oriented adjacent grains, and hence to accommodate the strain gradient in each grain, geometrically necessary dislocations form (additionally to the statistically stored dislocations). This results in a distribution of highly strained lattice domains in and around dislocation cores, and unstrained lattice in dislocation-free regions. These domains differ in both their thermodynamic and deformation behavior, thereby affecting kinetics and mass transport differently. This is discussed briefly below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge-transfer kinetics at unstrained vs. strained lattices
ce:section.ce:para:
It has been proposed that dislocations influence the charge-transfer kinetics via (1) effective increase in vacant sites (
θ ˆ
V
) via lattice expansion and is given by 31
(Equation 3)
θ ˆ
V
=
e
−
H V
R T
+ α
Ω
L i
(
ρ
b 2
)
Ω V
where H V is the enthalpy of vacancy formation, Ω Li and Ω V are the molar volume of lithium and vacancies, respectively, ρ is the dislocation density, α is a constant as explained in Equation 2, b is the magnitude of the Burgers vector of the dislocation, R and T are universal gas constant and absolute temperature, respectively. The second term on the right-hand side of Equation 3 accounts for the additional space created due to the lattice expansion around the dislocations. Charge-transfer is also affected by (2) the lattice distortion that enhances the chemical potential of the neighboring lithium atoms.

The charge-transfer resistance in the presence of dislocations is then given by the relationship:
(4)
R
C T
=
R
0 , C T
θ ˆ
V

e

(
1 − β
)
H V
R T
where R CT and R 0,CT are the charge-transfer resistances in the presence and absence of dislocations and applied stress, respectively, and β is the symmetry factor. It can be shown that, for β = 0.5, R CT < R 0,CT for increasing dislocation density. Thus, charge-transfer barriers are reduced in the strained regions surrounding dislocations.
Therefore, by invoking plastic anisotropy or a spatially varying dislocation distribution will result in inhomogeneous or non-uniform stripping across the interface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mass-transport in unstrained vs. strained lattice regions
ce:section.ce:para:
The non-zero hydrostatic stress component surrounding edge dislocations results in enhanced mass transport through these regions and is referred to as dislocation pipe diffusion (D d) and is usually 3–4 orders faster compared with bulk (unstrained) lattice diffusion (D V). 50
,
67 Therefore, lattice regions that are associated with high charge-transfer resistances are also associated with slow mass transport and vice versa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relative strengths (σ y) of unstrained vs. strained lattice regions
ce:section.ce:para: As a corollary to plastic anisotropy, a local variation of strength caused by the distribution of dislocation density is likely to occur. 68 Unstrained lattice regions will have lower strength compared with strained lattice regions.

Thus, it can be summarized that those regions surrounding the dislocation core are associated with low charge-transfer barriers, faster transport, and higher strength, whereas the opposite holds true in dislocation-free or unstrained lattice regions.

Charge-transfer across the interface can only be accomplished through contacted regions that are in optimum range of interaction. 60
,
69 In this section, we briefly discuss the factors that enable this.

Interfacial stabilization energy (γ LiǀLLZO) or equivalently the work of adhesion (W adh for LiǀLLZO is 0.67 J m−2) inhibits the formation of free surfaces. 61
,
70 Therefore, adhesion counteracts pore formation during stripping. 71

Creep is time-dependent plastic deformation occurring below yield stress and at higher homologous temperatures, i.e., T H > 0.3–0.4. 72 Lithium undergoes significant creep under ambient conditions, i.e., at T H = 0.65 and σ = 0.1 MPa. It has been shown that dislocation climb creep is the predominant deformation mechanism in bulk Li under the above conditions. 73
,
74 However, for strained electrodes, dislocation pipe diffusion-assisted Nabarro-Herring creep and Coble creep will also contribute to the strain rates and deformation. 75

During later stages, inhomogeneous stripping will lead to higher interfacial contact stresses or stress amplification leading to higher strain rates either via power-law breakdown or glide-assisted plastic deformation. 67

The combination of the above factors, outlined schematically in Figure 8 , is now used to propose a mechanism of pore formation in LMAs. During fabrication, the microstructural energy (elastic strain energy) of the final electrodes, i.e., SG-/LG-Li, increases on account of strain hardening (Figures 8A and 8B). 30
,
67 This is illustrated in Figure 8B, where the increase in dislocation density is shown by a nodal network of dislocations. Also shown are the dislocation rearrangements. Although the vacancy concentration (indicated by the dotted circles in Figure 8B) is also expected to increase, the high homologous temperature of Li indicates that these vacancies will quickly attain thermal equilibrium. Plastic anisotropy during fabrication results in local distribution of the strained lattice around the dislocation cores and the unstrained or normal lattice (Figures 8A and 8B).

During stripping, preferential electrodissolution of the strained lattice occurs, thus a lag between stripping rates is initiated. Higher charge-transfer resistance combined with lower strength (σ y) in the unstrained regions tends to favor a longer contact time. This is further supported by interfacial adhesion and favored creep in these regions. Therefore, these interfaces help the lithium to respond to the imposed current via electrodissolution of strained lattice via surface transport, as shown in Figure 8C. Thus, pore formation occurs for one thermodynamic and one kinetic reason: (1) reduction of stored elastic strain energy and (2) it allows lithium to utilize the fastest mass transport pathway, i.e., surface diffusion (as self-diffusivities follow the trend, D S ≥ D GB ≥ D D > D V). In addition, surface diffusion has been shown to be further favored over the dislocation-terminated surface regions. 32 Interestingly, the effect of electric field-assisted surface migration can be ignored as it has been shown to have negligible effect on Li transport. 76

This explains the combined effect of prolonged contact, fast mass transport pathways, and reduction of microstructural energy on the observed longer stripping time >13 h (Figure 3B). However, as the pores grow deeper and wider, mass transport via surface diffusion becomes progressively slower, as the diffusion lengths increase. This is shown by the curved black arrows in Figures 8C and 8D. The sloping pore surface along with deeper pores results in decreasing mass transport per unit length and time, assuming surface diffusivity is independent of surface defect concentration (characteristic diffusion length,
L =
π
D s
t
remains constant, where
D s
is the surface diffusivity and
t
is the time). 50 This leads to progressive accumulation of vacancies or reduced interfacial contact as evident from increasing potential in Figure 3B.

Apart from preferential stripping of the strained lattice regions to minimize their microstructural energy, the growing pores can also serve as annihilation sites for dislocations in their vicinity. This is illustrated in Figure 8D. The high homologous temperature of lithium leads to microstructural reorganization by recovery (annihilation of dislocations of opposite sign and rearrangement into a low energy dislocation structure [LEDS] 30 during polygonization in plastically bent crystals) and recrystallization (growth of sub-boundaries), resulting in softening. 38 A representative encounter of LEDS near the growing pore is shown in Figure 8D. The pore surfaces act as annihilation sites for LEDS, which leads to reduced mass transport per unit length and time via the roughening of the pore surface (Figure 8D). In addition, the roughening of the pore surface can also occur via continuous surface rearrangement of atoms to obtain low surface energy facets ((100) for lithium). 77 This process is indicated by the curved red arrow in Figure 8C, which indicates mass transport away from the interface. These factors lead to a progressive accumulation of vacancies, resulting in a sharp increase in potential at higher times (Figure 3B). It is worth noting that, at higher times, stress amplification at the depleted contact point will maintain interfacial contact either by power-law breakdown or slip-mediated plastic yielding (Figure 8D). 67 However, these faster deformation paths are unable to cope up with the imposed current, as mass transport becomes increasingly difficult as the pore grows, both in depth and width (Figure 3B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlating pore morphology with dislocation topology
ce:section.ce:para:
It can be summarized that the observed pore morphology (Figures 4 and 5) can be correlated with the topology of the dislocation (forest dislocations comprising nodal network and dislocation loops) 67
,
78 and plastic anisotropy, introduced during electrode processing. This is evident from the observed SEM, AFM, and confocal images (Figures 4 and 5), indicating different and distinct surface topographical features of the stripped LMAs. It is very difficult to predict the topologies of dislocations inside a plastically deformed grain, and this is strongly influenced by grain size. Nevertheless, Figures 4A and 4B suggest that stripped surfaces possibly resemble the geometry of Frank-Read type (concentric dislocation loops), whereas Figures 4C and 4D indicate relatively uniform stripping on account of higher dislocation density, as expected based on Equation 2. Furthermore, the fabrication process can be considered as hot working (as T H > 0.5) and Li is expected to undergo dynamic recovery/recrystallization during this step leading to further microstructural rearrangement, i.e., subgrain formation, recrystallized grains near high-angle GB, etc., 38 and is discussed next. Thereafter, correlation between stripped morphology and topology will be revisited.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructural changes on annealing at T H ≈ 0.99
ce:section.ce:para: Figures 9A, 9B, 9E, and 9F show the SEM images of the stripped h-LG-Li and h-SG-Li anodes, respectively, indicating the presence of random pits. Interestingly, the density of such pits is higher in h-LG-Li compared with h-SG-Li. Figure 9C shows a confocal image of the stripped h-LG-Li surface. The profile along a representative plane (indicated in blue in Figure 9C) passing through a pit is shown in Figure 9D. The profile indicates the pit depth to be ∼20 μm with a sloping side-wall profile. Similar analyses for h-SG-Li are shown in Figures 9G and 9H. The pit depth is revealed to be ∼50 μm with a serrated sloping side wall profile.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Softening mechanism: Recrystallization and grain growth
ce:section.ce:para: Thermal treatment of SG-Li and LG-Li (Figure 1; see supplemental experimental procedures for details) at T H = 0.99 lead to microstructural recrystallization and eventually to grain coarsening. Since it was not possible to observe the microstructure of h-SG/h-LG-Li, SEM analyses were performed on the blank heated fine-/coarse-grained foils (see supplemental information for details) and the conclusions were correlated with the former. It must be noted that recrystallization/grain growth dynamics in SG-/LG-Li and blank heated foils on annealing are expected to be different on account of differences in microstructural energy (absence of significant strain hardening) and presence of interfaces (i.e., LLZO and Cu) in the former.

SEM images (Figure S2 reveal that a heated fine-grained foil (a microstructural representative for SG-Li leading to h-SG-Li) exhibits a high degree of grain coarsening (20 μm → 120 μm) compared with a coarse-grained sample (160 μm → 215 μm). This is attributed to the relative differences in the stored microstructural energy (Equation 2) and the fraction of high-angle GBs in the samples.

Figures 9I–9L schematically depict the microstructural evolution of SG-Li/LG-Li on annealing leading to h-SG-/h-LG-Li. Figure 9I shows that both SG-Li and LG-Li have uniform dislocation distribution (for simplicity we ignore the effect of dynamic recovery/recrystallization). 30
,
38 Initial stages of annealing leads to microstructural rearrangement via mutual annihilation of opposite signed dislocations and rearrangement of the similarly oriented dislocations into LEDS during polygonization (Figures 9J and 9K). During recrystallization, nucleation occurs in the regions of strong lattice curvature or high-lattice-strain energy, i.e., slip line intersections, in the vicinity of grain boundaries, etc. LEDS or the sub-boundaries then coalesce during subgrain growth during recrystallization and form part of the deformed matrix with the simultaneous formation of strain-free matrix (Figure 9K).

Since the grain size of the recrystallized microstructure depends only on the state of microstructural energy, the greater degree of grain coarsening observed in the annealed fine-grained sample agrees well with Equation 2. It indicates higher microstructural energy in the form of higher dislocation density and higher fraction of high-angle GBs (kinetically trapped system) compared with the coarse-grained sample. In combination with the greater mobility of high-angle GBs, this leads to greater microstructural rearrangements during annealing in the fine-grained microstructure. 38 The corresponding driving force is lower for the coarse-grained samples during annealing as they have a higher fraction of low-angle GBs and a lower dislocation density.

Grain growth follows recrystallization and is driven mainly by grain boundary surface energies, where the system strives to achieve a smaller grain boundary area with a higher number of facets, i.e., six or seven (driven by GB curvature). This can be seen in Figure 9L, where the new microstructure is represented by black lines superimposed on the pre-annealed microstructure shown in gray.

Therefore, recrystallization consumes strained lattice regions (acting as nuclei) and integrates LEDS as subgrain boundaries, but certain complex dislocation arrangements, i.e., forest dislocations that either newly form or interact with pre-existing dislocations during annealing, remain immobile (sessile), as shown in Figure 9L. Following discussion in “pore formation: a multi-factorial effect,” it can be concluded that these regions are responsible for the observed pits, as shown in Figures 9A–9H. The lower driving force in the coarse-grained sample during annealing also explains that these immobile, strained regions exhibit lower mobility, and that microstructural regeneration leads to the formation of a larger number of these domains. This explains the greater density of the pits observed in the annealed coarse-grained sample (Figure 9A).

Recrystallization is favored in fine-grained samples on account of the higher fraction of high-angle boundaries as they have higher mobility, which is given by 38
(Equation 5)
V = M P
where V, M, and P are GB velocity, mobility, and net pressure across the GB, respectively. P consists of contributions from (1)
P
S E
, the stored energy difference between adjacent lattice, and (2)
P C
, the pressure due to GB curvature, and is expressed as
(Equation 6)
P =
P
S E
+
P C
= α ρ G
b 2
+
2
γ b
r
where
γ b
and
r
are GB surface energy and curvature, respectively. Clearly,
ρ
is higher in the fine-grained sample. Also, the finer microstructure also inherits a larger fraction of grains with a lower number of sides (5–3) having higher GB curvature. This additionally contributes to greater microstructural rearrangement in annealed fine-grained microstructure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge extraction and time to reach the cutoff potential
ce:section.ce:para: The maximum capacity before depletion or the time to reach the cutoff potential (1.0 V) can now be discussed in terms of the length of the dislocation network, the density, and the plastic anisotropy affecting deformation. A uniform dislocation distribution with high density in a coarse-grained sample (higher dislocation length) leads to higher capacity (∼0.60 mAh for LG-Li), although the electrode roughness is higher (Figures 10 and 5E). On the other hand, a uniform dislocation distribution with high density in a fine-grained sample (lower dislocation length) leads to a relatively low capacity (∼0.36 mAh for LG-Li) but with relatively smoother stripped electrode (Figures 10 and 5G). Therefore, it can be concluded that capacity scales with the grain size and hence the dislocation length, as is evident when comparing SG-Li with h-SG-Li (∼0.41 mAh) and h-LG-Li (0.46 mAh). This correlates with the total percentage of anode utilization and is found to be around 10.3%, 8.0%, 6.3%, and 7.0% for LG-Li, h-LG-Li, SG-Li, and h-SG-Li, respectively (calculated assuming an electrode thickness of 100 μm and diameter of 6 mm with a capacity of 5.816 mAh). This is shown in Figure 10. The right vertical axis indicates the theoretical thickness or the height (in μm ) of lithium removed corresponding to the extracted capacity assuming layer-by-layer lithium removal.

The available capacity before depletion is aided by lattice deformability. This is evident from the slope of the potential profile, i.e., E vs. t. At longer time, this is an indicator of relative strengths as this regime is marked by interfacial stress amplification. This is evident for LG-Li and is more pronounced for h-LG-Li, both of which indicate greater slope compared with SG-Li and h-SG-Li.

As a corollary to previous discussions, the strength of the annealed electrodes is expected to be lower, i.e., σ y (SG-Li) > σ y (LG-Li) > σ y (h-SG-Li), σ y (h-LG-Li). Hence, a lower strength allows pronounced stress amplification and deformation observed for h-LG-Li.

We show that the microstructure of the LMA strongly influences the morphology of stripped LMAs. A 3-fold control over the dislocation “inventory” of the LMA, i.e., dislocation length, density, and distribution in the LMAs by appropriate thermo-mechanical processing, suggests that dislocations are possibly related to the observed inhomogeneous stripping during anodic discharge in SSBs. Moreover, they determine the stripping capacity before depletion occurs. A more uniform dislocation distribution with higher density in a coarse-grained sample (larger dislocation length) leads to higher stripping capacity, although the electrode roughness is higher. On the other hand, a uniform dislocation distribution with higher density in a fine-grained sample (shorter dislocation length) results in relatively lower stripping capacity but with a relatively smoother stripped electrode. Our operando impedance measurements were corroborated with SEM, confocal microscopy, and AFM measurements. Pore formation is a multi-factorial process, and any proposed mechanism must account for factors such as enhanced kinetics at dislocations, dislocation length, plastic anisotropy, adhesion, dynamic recovery/recrystallization, creep, surface diffusion, etc. Clearly, pore formation in LMA helps to deliver more charge during stripping, as this is the fastest mass transport pathway that a system develops in response to applied current; however, at the cost of severe morphological change.

We then investigated the effect of pore morphology on the stress required to re-form interfacial contact. We show that, at the highest pressure studied (3.0 MPa), the formation of interfacial contact is independent of pore morphology and depends instead on the initial electrode strength, i.e., grain boundary strengthening and grain boundary-induced strain hardening. Although a higher contact area was obtained for highly roughened electrodes, i.e., LG-Li, indicating higher deformability, these are only first cycle measurements. Long-term cycling effects must be considered to understand microstructure-stress relationship, as Li has a bcc structure that is extremely sensitive to strain rates and a transition from ductile to brittle is very likely. This would further increase the required stack pressure thereby affecting local current distribution and hence CCD.
Further information and requests for resources and reagents should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Jürgen Janek (Juergen.Janek@pc.jlug.de).
This study did not generate new unique materials.
